exemplar.title: How does the temperature (5.0, 15.0, 25.0, 35.0, 45.0)â¦C affect the rate constant of the sulfuric acid catalysed iodination of propan-2-one, as measured by a colorimeter at 470 nm, and how can this relationship be used to measure the activation energy of the reaction?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the temperature (5.0, 15.0, 25.0, 35.0, 45.0)â¦C affect the rate constant of the sulfuric acid catalysed iodination of propan-2-one, as measured by a colorimeter at 470 nm, and how can this relationship be used to measure the activation energy of the reaction?

strengths: Data tables and graphs are communicated clearly and precisely with correct units, labels, significant figures and error bars
Appropriate treatment of mean rates, standard deviations, regression and activation energy calculation
weaknesses: Instrumental uncertainty of the colorimeter dismissed without justification
Uncertainty propagation for gradient and activation energy lacks methodological detail
Inconsistent significant-figure usage in raw data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While data communication and processing are strong (band 5â6 strands), significant omissions in the consideration and propagation of uncertainties (band 3â4 strand) limit this criterion to the 3â4 band. Consequently, a holistic score of 4 reflects the need for more rigorous uncertainty treatment despite clear presentation and largely accurate processing.

strengths: Clear and specific research question framed in an appropriate chemical context
Detailed justification of temperature range, catalyst, reagents and detection wavelength
Step-by-step procedure with concentrations and volumes enabling replication
Acknowledgement of measurement uncertainties in burette, thermometer and data-logger
weaknesses: Heat-loss management in custom water bath not fully resolved
Timing between reagent mixing and measurement start not precisely defined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research Design demonstrates a fully appropriate context (band 6 strand) and highly reproducible methodology (band 5 strand) with detailed variables and controls (band 5 strand). Minor ambiguities around heat loss mitigation and exact mixing timing prevent the absolute top mark, so a 5 accurately reflects the strong but not flawless design.

strengths: Conclusion is fully consistent with analysis, restating the linear Arrhenius relationship and reporting Ea with quantified uncertainty
Calculates and reports percentage error against literature value, demonstrating contextual awareness
Acknowledges potential underestimation of uncertainties as caveats
weaknesses: Omission of uncertainty range for the reference literature value in comparison
Discussion of differences remains qualitative rather than exploring combined uncertainty bounds
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified and aligns with the data analysis, with relevant comparison to literature (both band-5 strands). Minor depth missing in the contextual uncertainty discussion prevents the very top mark, so a score of 5 is merited.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses and their relative impacts are described, with classification of systematic vs random errors
Relevant and realistic improvements are proposed for each limitation and linked to error reduction mechanisms
weaknesses: Impact descriptions remain qualitative without quantitative weighting of error sources
No feasibility or cost analysis for suggested improvements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation identifies and explains the impact of methodological weaknesses and proposes relevant improvements (both band-5 strands). The lack of quantitative magnitudes for error sources and discussion of feasibility prevents a top-band score, so a 5 reflects a strong but not exhaustive evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames a clearly focused question, specifying the reaction, catalyst, temperature range and measurable output (colour at 470 nm).  The introduction provides chemical background (clock reaction, mechanism, Arrhenius theory) and explains why the study is worthwhile (link to activation energy determination).  This situates the question in a specific and fully appropriate chemical context, matching the descriptor for the top band.

Mean rates, standard deviations, propagation to k, ln k, 1/T, linear regression and Ea calculation are correct and internally consistent.  One outlier is identified and rationalised.  Minor weaknesses (sign error discussion not explicit, limited data set) prevent a 6, but the processing remains appropriate and largely accurate, meriting 5.

Data tables have clear headings, correct units, appropriate significant figures and standard deviations.  Graphs include axes labels, units, error bars and equation/RÂ².  Commentary explains rounding conventions.  Both clarity and precision are consistently demonstrated, meeting the 5-6 descriptor; no significant lapses are evident, justifying the top score of 6.

For every limitation the student proposes realistic, relevant improvements (commercial water-bath, same-day trials, darker room, gradual heating, post-mix temperature measurement).  Explanations link directly to how each fix would reduce the specific error source.  The depth is good but not exhaustive (no cost/feasibility analysis), so 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Thermometer and burette uncertainties, propagated % uncertainties, error bars and discussion of increasing SD are treated well.  However the colourimeterâs instrumental uncertainty is dismissed as âcould not be determinedâ, and uncertainty in gradient/activation energy is extracted from Excel without method detail.  These omissions are significant but not crippling, placing the work in the 3-4 band; the stronger aspects justify the upper value 4.

Comparison with a peer-reviewed literature value (88.5 kJ molâ»Â¹) and calculation of 14 % percentage error shows relevant contextualisation.  Discussion of why the value differs (systematic errors, equipment limits) is present but not deeply quantitative, so the work is well-justified but not exceptionalâband 5.

The evaluation table identifies heat loss, environmental variation, photodecomposition of iodine, boiling of propanone, and timing errors, each categorised as systematic or random with a concise explanation of how they influence rate or Ea.  The relative impact is discussed qualitatively (e.g., âminimalâ vs âsignificantâ) but lacks quantitative weighting, so a strong yet not perfect explanation earns 5.

Variables, controls and data-collection strategy are laid out in detail: five temperatures, five trials each, justification of 10 Â°C steps, zero-order iodine allowing colourimetry, rationale for 470 nm, and control table for concentrations, mixing, airflow, wavelength etc.  Uncertainties in burettes, thermometer, and data-logger are acknowledged.  While largely thorough, heat-loss management is not completely resolved, so the treatment is strong but not flawless, warranting a 5 rather than 6.

The step-by-step method (13 numbered steps), diagrams of each apparatus set-up and concentrations/volumes enable replication.  Minor ambiguities remain (exact timing between mixing and cuvette insertion, details of tangent tool settings), so the description is highly reproducible but not absolutely exhaustive; 5 is appropriate.

The conclusion restates the linear Arrhenius relationship and reports Ea with quantified uncertainty, explicitly linking to the data analysis and graph.  Consistency between gradient and calculated activation energy is demonstrated.  Minor caveats about potential underestimation of uncertainties are noted but not fully resolved, hence 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The introduction would benefit from an explicit hypothesis statement to make clear the expected temperatureârate constant relationship before experimental details.
Citation âRate equationsâ lacks author and year details; ensure all references include full bibliographic information for clarity and traceability.
The four-step mechanism is presented with clear chemical equations and well-labeled figures, enhancing conceptual understanding.
Description of electron shifts in Step II is somewhat ambiguous; consider adding a curly-arrow diagram to clarify intermediate structure changes.
Including a reaction energy profile diagram would strengthen the theoretical framework by visualizing activation energy and intermediate energies.
Control description for mixing implies âno mixingââthis may lead to inconsistent distribution of reactants and affect reproducibility.

The research question is clearly stated within a specific chemical context, including reaction details, catalyst, temperature range and detection wavelength, which grounds the investigation appropriately.
The independent variable (temperature range) is well justified with both practical and theoretical limits, supporting reproducibility.
Custom water bath design omits quantitative control of temperature fluctuations during mixing, potentially introducing systematic heat-loss errors.
To mitigate heat-loss effects, consider adding a lid or insulating jacket to the water bath and monitoring temperature throughout each trial.
Control of catalyst type, concentration, airflow and wavelength is comprehensive, demonstrating thoughtful methodological planning.
Document the exact mixing procedure and time interval between reagent addition and measurement to enable full method reproduction.
The preparation of a 0.0050 mol dmâ»Â³ iodine solution is described step-by-step with rationale, aiding replicability.
The numbered procedure list (steps 1â13) is detailed and sequential, facilitating replication by future researchers.
Timing between reactant mixing and cuvette insertion is not quantified, allowing uncontrolled variation in initial rate measurement.
Specify the target lag time (e.g., 5 s) between mixing and measurement start to standardize initial rate determination.
Raw data table includes standard deviations, demonstrating clear and precise communication of data spread.
Apply the root-sum-of-squares method for propagation of volume uncertainties to obtain ÎV_total more rigorously.
Error bars and linear fit on ln(k) vs. 1/T graph are presented clearly, aiding visual assessment of data quality.
Discussion conflates absolute and fractional uncertainties; clarify whether error bars reflect Â±Ï or Â±% to avoid confusion.
Literature comparison omits the uncertainty range of the reference value when assessing agreement; include both error margins for rigor.
Custom water bath lacks continuous temperature logging; introduce a second thermometer in the reaction mixture to verify isothermal conditions.
Negative values in rate table should be explained in the caption, clarifying that sign indicates decrease in absorbance.
Decimal places in raw rate values vary (4â5 dp), reducing consistency and precision; standardize rounding before analysis.
Round all initial rates to a consistent number of significant figures prior to computing means and standard deviations.
Calculation of k with uncertainty is clearly laid out, showing proper use of rate law rearrangement and uncertainty propagation.
Uncertainty in V_total calculated by simple summation of absolute uncertainties rather than propagation of variances may under- or overestimate ÎV.
Revise the narrative to differentiate between absolute error bar lengths and their percentage uncertainties for clearer interpretation.
Marking the 35.0 Â°C point as an outlier is reasonable, but applying a statistical test (e.g., Grubbs') would strengthen justification.
Calculated activation energy is well presented with uncertainty, and comparison to literature demonstrates context awareness.
Consider integrating measurement of the Arrhenius pre-exponential factor A within future experiments by fitting k vs T data directly.
Discuss whether the experimentally determined Ea falls within combined uncertainty bounds when compared to literature values.
Evaluation tables systematically identify limitations and improvements with systematic vs random impact classifications.
Impact descriptions remain qualitative; incorporating approximate magnitudes for each error source would clarify their relative significance.
Quantify error source magnitudes (e.g., % heat loss) to prioritize methodological improvements based on their actual impact.
The conclusion succinctly restates the linear ln(k)â1/T relationship and outcome, confirming the study aim was achieved.
Future work section identifies varied catalysts and halogens but could more directly link to unresolved systematic uncertainties in this study.
Could the experimental setup be modified to determine d and Î¼ from the extended Arrhenius equation, thereby quantifying systematic uncertainty?
